title: if Statement
description: The syntax and mechanics of if: braces, single-statement bodies, and the = vs == mistake in a real condition.
objectives: Write a basic if statement with correct syntax
Understand when braces are required vs optional
Trace exactly how an if statement executes

sections.text: The condition goes inside parentheses. If it evaluates to true, the block inside the braces runs. If it's false, the block is skipped entirely.

sections.title: Tracing it
points: The block inside { } runs, printing You are an adult.
If age had been 15, the condition would be false and nothing would print

sections.text: 3. Braces Are Optional for a Single Statement

If the body is exactly one statement, C++ allows dropping the braces. This is legal, but it's a common source of bugs — if someone later adds a second line expecting it to be "inside" the if without also adding braces, only the first line is actually conditional. Using braces even for one-line bodies is a widely recommended habit.
Indentation is just for readability to humans — it has no effect on which lines are actually inside the if. Only the braces (or the single next statement, with none) decide that.

Recall from Operators: = assigns, == compares. if (a = 5) assigns 5 to a and then checks whether the result (5) is truthy — which it always is, since 5 is non-zero. The block runs every single time, regardless of a's original value, and a has now been silently overwritten. The correct condition is if (a == 5).
A condition can be any bool expression — including a bool variable used directly, without needing == true. if (isLoggedIn) already means "if this is true."

An if can contain another if inside its block — the inner condition is only even checked if the outer one was true. We'll see cleaner ways to combine conditions once we cover && in more depth, but nesting like this is valid and common.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Writing if (a = 5) instead of if (a == 5) — this assigns instead of comparing, and the block runs almost unconditionally.
Assuming indentation controls what's inside the if — only braces (or the single next statement) do.
Adding a second statement after a brace-less if, expecting it to be conditional too — without braces, only the very next statement is inside the if.
Writing if (isLoggedIn == true) — redundant; if (isLoggedIn) already checks the same thing.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is the basic syntax of an if statement?
questions.answer: if (condition) { ... } — the block runs only when the condition evaluates to true.

questions.question: Are braces required for an if statement?
questions.answer: Only if the body has more than one statement. A single-statement body can omit braces, though using them anyway is a recommended habit to avoid bugs.

questions.question: What's wrong with if (a = 5)?
questions.answer: It uses assignment (=) instead of comparison (==), so it assigns 5 to a and the condition becomes true almost unconditionally, since a non-zero value is truthy.

questions.question: Can an if condition be a bool variable directly?
questions.answer: Yes — if (isLoggedIn) is valid and equivalent to if (isLoggedIn == true), but simpler.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: An if statement runs a block of code only when its condition evaluates to true: if (condition) { ... }. Braces are required for multi-statement bodies but optional for a single statement, though omitting them is a common source of bugs. A classic mistake is writing if (a = 5), which assigns instead of comparing due to confusing = with ==, making the condition almost always true. A condition can be any bool expression, including a bool variable used directly, and if statements can be nested inside one another.

sections.title: Trick to Remember if
sections.text: if (condition) → true runs the block, false skips it. Always == to compare, never = inside a condition. When in doubt, use braces.
